speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Pamela Freeman. Today's date is November 21, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Turn ?] [? it down. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Elizabeth Gorski, in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hang on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Elizabeth Gorski, G-O-R-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth, the Polish name is Elzbieta, that I've anglicized here to Elizabeth. And my second name was Fizhiut, F-I-Z-H-I-U-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is fifth November, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age today is 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin by asking you about your childhood. Can you tell me about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would like to take a-- tell a few words about my grandparents. My paternal grandparents, I hardly knew the grandfather. He died when I was about three or four years old. All I remember is a little old man with a small, white beard. He used to be a bank employee. And they had, years before the war, converted to Protestantism, because that enabled him to advance in his work. They had three boys. The oldest, Joseph, was a bachelor, living with grandmother all his life. The second [? aged, ?] he was my father. And the youngest, Roman, was also a bachelor. Roman worked in public service before the war. Roman had converted with his parents, because he wanted to advance at work. Joseph didn't. Neither did my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My grandmother ran a frock salon in her apartment. She had quite elegant ladies coming, to be dressed there. She had two or three seamstresses working. And the-- the forelady, a Polish girl called Jadwiga Giegiecka. She'll be quite important in the further story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The oldest son, Joseph, was madly in love with her. She was quite a good-looking young woman. But of course, he couldn't have married her. Grandmother would never, ever agree to such a mismatch. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When my brother was born-- he is older-- he was older than I-- my grandparents wanted him to be converted. But my parents hesitated. They were totally irreligious and non-traditional. But they didn't want to decide his future, as a baby. They decided to let him grow up and make a decision by himself. Well, he was never eventually given the chance to do that. But because they wanted to give him a free hand, they didn't circumcise him. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Which could have helped him to survive, but didn't, due to the circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Out of my paternal family, I'm the only survivor. There's nobody else left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The older uncle perished during the war. The younger one survived. Him and my grandmother were sheltered by Jadwiga's family, in the country. Grandma died there, peacefully in her sleep. And Roman, after the war, married Jadwiga Giegiecka. They lived like two lovebirds for a number of years afterwards. But it was too late for them to have any children anyway. My maternal family was quite extensive. I never knew the grandfather. He died some years before I was born. He was, apparently, quite a wealthy merchant. My grandmother had five children, four girls and one boy. The girls were all very good-looking, and very strong women. There is, in the family of my grandmother, the Markson family, a tradition of tough, strong women. The one boy was not quite such a success. The eldest daughter, Anna, finished medical studies after World War I. She was a doctor. She married a doctor and lived in [? Lodz. ?] The second daughter, [? Helen, ?] was my mother. She had two children, my brother and myself. She almost completed chemistry studies in [? Kharkiv. ?] But she was too heavily pregnant with my brother to sit for final exams. And she never, never, ever worked in this area. The third daughter, Maria, [? Maricia, ?] as we called her, was gorgeous. She was very fair, blue-eyed, lovely smile. She was gentle. She was a sports woman. She used-- used to take us kids ice skating in winter, horse riding in summer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was also a doctor. And she married a doctor. She had one daughter, Jan Karol, nine years my junior. Then there was the boy, Aleksander. He'd been sent to friends, to study. And he stayed there. He was an engineer. Somehow or else, he couldn't make a decent living. Their mother helped him all-- all of her life. They used to send their two girls for holidays to us. Because they couldn't give them a decent holiday. I really met him only after the war. And I'll come to that later. The youngest girl, Eugenia-- we called her [? Gilka-- ?] was lovely. She played the violin. She was a kindergarten teacher. She used to sing with us and play us little songs, and dance around. She got married in the late '30s. She had a little girl called Wanda, born on the 26th of August, 1939, six days before the war. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would like to-- here, to say a couple gems about the Markson family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a story that our great-great grandfather-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry, I'll stop here and start again. The family came from the northeast of Poland, Wilno and surrounds. It's now Lithuania. It used to be Poland, before the war. There was a story that our great-great grandfather, early in the last century, had a print shop, printed Polish books, which was really quite exceptional at the time. There was also a story about a great-great grandmother who sat in the marketplace at Suwalki, near Wilno, selling love potion. So the family was quite extensive, as I said. And we spent quite a bit of time together, even though three of the sisters lived in Warsaw and one in [? Lodz, ?] what we used to visit each other. All the tradition I knew as a young child was my grandmother's Passover dinner. It was not really a traditional dinner but just, more or less, a family gathering. We were all there that day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I grew up in absolute ignorance of my origin. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I just-- I guess my parents did not think it important enough to tell me that I was Jewish. There was no religion at home. We had a nanny who used to take me, sometimes, to the church. But that meant nothing to me, at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And not until I went to school, aged seven, and I was asked the question, what's my religion? That was a question you had to answer in Poland in those times, wherever you went, in any documents. And I said, I have no religion. And the teacher ask again, what is your religion? I said, no madam. I have no religion. Well, then, you must be Jewish. And I came home in tears. Mommy, is it true that I am Jewish? They told me this at school. And I think they hurt me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Other than that, my childhood was quite a normal, happy childhood. My father was an employee of the Warsaw agency of Michelin Tires. That was a French company. And because I was the apple of Father's eye, Father decided that I will go to France to study, when I grow up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after I finished-- oh, not even full primary school. After I finished five years of primary school, I was sent to a French school in Warsaw called Lycee Francais, to get to know the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went there two years, between grades six and seven, and until the war, until 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must have had some aptitude for languages, something. Because I knew hardly any French when I started. And all-- all classes were conducted in French, only. After the first year, I walked out with three prizes for various subjects. The prizes were always books. After the second year, I walked off with a heavy armful of prizes. But then, of course, the war broke out. And that ended my French studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me more about your-- the first school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first school I went to was a school at the-- sort of connected to the Protestant Church, in Warsaw. My parents thought that the school would-- would be more tolerant. Because all state schools were basically Catholic schools. And they didn't want to send me to a Jewish school. I knew no Yiddish. And I-- we were really far away from that. I have little memories from that. I remember one or two girls. I was a-- I remember that I was a very untidy writer. And I had continuous problems with the teachers, over my-- my copy books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what subjects you were learning at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the usual elementary school subjects, Polish, arithmetic, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] art, physical education. There-- there was very, very little to-- my memories of that school are rather vague. And not-- not terribly-- --pleasant, if I can say that. I was feeling a bit of stranger, there. I was not really persecuted in any way. I was not laughed at or anything. But I wasn't really sitting well in this-- in-- in this school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish students at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, not that I knew of. I never knew of any others there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any of the games that used to play in the school yard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the normal girls' games that-- that all girls play, everywhere in the world. There was nothing very special, sort of connected-- connecting me to this school. My memories of the French school are much clearer and sort of more pleasant, fonder. I remember the teachers. I remember writing some stupid notes about one of our teacher and being called, and my parents being called for it-- for it, talking over. And I remember an old Latin teacher falling. It was the school occupied the whole building. And somebody threw a banana skin in the stairs. And the old teachers fell and broke his leg. And he'd never come back teaching, after that. I remember plays that we'd set up there. And that was a very pleasant time in my life. And I sort of enjoyed going there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The school catered mostly for children of businessmen and diplomats. They created it in Warsaw. There were some local children, also. But I have quite pleasant memories from it. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you felt that you belonged there more than at the previous school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I felt that I belonged there, more than to the previous school. We were-- in Poland then, and even now, I think-- very much French-oriented, as far as the cultural orientation. And because my parent-- my father worked for a French company, it all sort of fitted in together. We were there, taught in the French system, which involved a lot of memorizing of very long texts, rather than understanding what we were talking about. But because I had good memory, it fitted me to perfection. And it actually was a good exercise, for memory. And I really liked that time. I-- I have quite you know, pleasant memories of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your school friends from then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few. Very few. I remember some faces, but no names that I could attach to them. I have not kept any friendships from that time. Maybe because when the war started it broke my education. And it broke all my-- --friendships and connections with other children. So I just never saw any of them after September, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about your family life as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a nanny when we were young children, called Marynia, who was a very gentle soul, and a very lovely lady. When we grew out of the need for her attention, she left us, and came back after a few years and asked to be allowed to stay as a housekeeper. And she stayed with us for still, quite a few years after. About a year before the war, she married. She wasn't quite that young anymore, then. She married a man who had a little shop on the outskirts of Warsaw. And one of the first bombs that fell on the city hit the shop and killed them both. Other than that, my childhood was quite peaceful and reasonably happy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was not very highly paid in his job. Mother never worked. She was running the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she had the need for some additional income. So for quite a number of years before the war, beginning at about me being aged six or so, she would organize summer camps for children. She would rent a house somewhere in the country, in the hills, or at the seaside.

speaker: interviewee
text: She would hire a cook and a sports instructor. And she would take a group of 10, 15, 20 children of her friends, who would pay for their-- their stay there. And so that we had a terrific time for two months of the summer holidays. And they were our-- these are my really fondest memories of my childhood, the summer holidays we had. We had ballgames. We made excursions. We helped in harvesting. We'd gone swimming. We'd-- we had our love affairs, age 10 or 12, our best friends. It was a great time. And it gave mother an extra income that would last her the year, just to supplement whatever dad had brought home.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home, what sort of pastimes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go out with father on Sundays. He loved to take us to the park. We lived in a flat where there was a-- one of the best Warsaw cake shops opposite. And I remember father going to buy a box of cakes, and carrying-- he picked up, with the string tied across his button. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a very pleasant man, good to be with. Unfortunately, his relations with my mother were not top. I didn't realize it as a child, at all. It didn't hit me until a lot later, when I saw other children's parents living together, sharing a bedroom, that my parents didn't. I didn't consider it, then, as anything unusual. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father travelled a lot. He had a separate bedroom. And usually I shared his bedroom and my brothers shared my mother's bedroom. I thought, then, that it was the usual course of events. I was very naive as a girl, really, at this age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there occasions when you all went out together, as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rare. We usually went out with dad on Sundays, with mother on weekdays, if we ever went out. Rarely as a family together. My parents rarely went out together. Mother had her circle of friends, her bridge. Father had his goings out. We never knew where. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and-- did you and your brother have a good relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was a jealous little bitch, at the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was more able, at school. I was learning quicker than him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was really quite-- well, he was very gentle with me. He felt he was older, and a lot stronger. And he felt he had to protect me. And I was really, very often, quite nasty. I would scratch and kick, and do all that, whatever little bitches do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was-- we both were-- learning to play the piano with Uncle Jozef, who was a bit of a piano teacher. But I was-- I wasn't making much of a progress, whereas my brother was very good at it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He was 14 when the war broke out. I was 12. He was a very big boy, then. He was tall, well built. He looked a lot older than that. You could give him 18. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I remember him. I never seen him afterwards. You said before, that you had-- and your family, there was no religion. But were you aware of any anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I was. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the events?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were times when-- I did not meet it at school. Neither in the first elementary school, nor in the French school. But it had happened a number of times that in the street, I was walking to a lesson, or wherever.

speaker: interviewee
text: A man had stopped me and said, [FRENCH]. Go back where you came from. And I would then run home, crying, to mother. What do I do? What sort of things would your mother say, when you told her? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her saying anything particular. She'd just calm me. Next time, she'd take me to the same spot, because I actually met the same man two or three times on the way to-- to a teacher I used to go to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from then on, she'd started taking me. She didn't want to let me walk on my own, there. She never-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --somehow, the Jewish question was not discussed at home. It was just left to run its course. It was not-- not a problem that was talked about . Did you ever ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew by then, I was Jewish. By then, I knew that I was Jewish. I couldn't do anything about. I was afraid of that guy. I was very much afraid of him. There was a time-- I have to go back a bit-- during the early '30s, during Depression, my father had lost his job. He was, then, scraping a living, playing the violin in silent movies. And we had, then, to move out from the flat we were living in, to a much poorer area in the Jewish area. And we had a very sort of small, miserable little flat. In the same house, there was a-- well, it was not a synagogue, really, as sort of a prayer house. And it was very, very strange to me, very strange. We lived there for some two or three years, before my dad got his job back again, at a much-reduced salary. And we moved into a better flat, in a better area of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just stop here for a tape break.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
